article_title: US Offshore Wind Survives Its Biggest Political Test as All Five Stop-Work Orders Fall in Court

content_preview.title: US Offshore Wind Survives Its Biggest Political Test as All Five Stop-Work Orders Fall in Court
content_preview.summary: Federal judges have struck down all five Trump administration stop-work orders on Atlantic offshore wind projects, clearing the way for 7.5 GW of capacity to power 2.5 million homes.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The United States offshore wind industry has cleared its most serious political obstacle to date. Between mid-January and early February 2026, four different federal judges in separate jurisdictions [struck down all five stop-work orders](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/02/us-court-again-rules-an-offshore-wind-project-can-resume-construction.html) that the Trump administration had imposed on Atlantic Coast wind projects in December 2025. With construction now resumed on every affected ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive Analysis article at 956 words covering the defeat of five offshore wind stop-work orders. Strong structure with detailed project-by-project breakdown and thoughtful analysis section.
editor_notes.source_verification: 5 sources, all captured: CNBC (source-0), Electrek (source-1), Utility Dive (source-2), CleanTechnica (source-3), Renewable Energy World (source-4). All 5 fully captured as HTML snapshots.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Verified claims: all five stop-work orders struck down with Sunrise Wind last on Feb 2 (Electrek), December 22 Interior Department suspension orders (Electrek), national security/radar justification (Electrek), CVOW 2.6 GW and >70% complete (Renewable Energy World), $228M stop-work cost increase (Renewable Energy World), $11.5B current budget (Renewable Energy World), 176 turbines (Renewable Energy World + Utility Dive), Charybdis Jones Act vessel (Renewable Energy World + CleanTechnica), first power by March (Renewable Energy World), Revolution Wind 350,000 homes (Electrek), Empire Wind 810 MW (CleanTechnica), Empire Wind >60% complete (Electrek), Maersk vessel from Singapore with 1,900-tonne crane for 15+ MW turbines (CleanTechnica), $2M/day Vineyard Wind savings during cold snap (Electrek). CONCERNS: (1) Revolution Wind completion: article says 'approximately 80%' but Electrek says '87% complete' — understated. (2) $137M tariff figure and $9.8B original budget not found in any captured snapshot — Utility Dive mentions $690M total tariffs and $11.2B at earlier date. (3) Vineyard Wind 95% and Sunrise Wind 45% completion percentages not verified in any captured source. (4) Sunrise Wind '600,000 homes' claim not found in snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Balanced and analytical. Presents both industry achievements and financial/political caveats. No advocacy or sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on offshore wind stop-work orders or CVOW specifically.
concerns: Revolution Wind completion understated: 80% in article vs 87% in Electrek source
$137M tariff figure and $9.8B original budget not verifiable in any captured source snapshot
Vineyard Wind 95%, Sunrise Wind 45% completion, and Sunrise Wind 600K homes claims lack source verification
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong energy policy Analysis with mostly verified claims. Core narrative (all five stop-work orders defeated in court) is fully verified. Several specific figures ($137M tariffs, $9.8B original budget, 95%/45% completion rates for Vineyard/Sunrise Wind) cannot be traced to captured sources — these may come from the CNBC article whose body renders as largely JavaScript/structured data. Revolution Wind completion understated (80% vs source's 87%). Despite these issues, the article's fundamental thesis and most key facts are well-sourced. Approved with noted concerns.
